agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quotes bounded context. However, the term 'confirmed' is already established in the Orders bounded context with a specific, well-defined meaning: paid and immutable. The new usage means something fundamentally different: verbally agreed but unpaid and mutable.","bounded_context":"Quotes (proposed change) vs Orders (existing definition)","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (PROPOSED — REJECTED)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. This is mutable (customer could still back out). This term CONFLICTS with the established meaning of 'confirmed'."},{"term":"Accepted Quote (RECOMMENDED ALTERNATIVE)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has not yet occurred. Clearly distinct from 'confirmed' in the Orders context."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A proposal of terms and pricing presented to a customer, subject to change until formalized into an order."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A formalized commitment to fulfill goods/services, progressing through statuses including 'confirmed' (paid, immutable)."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"The term 'confirmed' in the domain means PAID and IMMUTABLE. This is an established invariant in the Orders context.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Redefining 'confirmed' to mean 'verbally agreed, unpaid, mutable' in another context creates a dangerous semantic collision. Developers, support staff, analysts, and automated systems that key on the word 'confirmed' will conflate the two meanings, leading to incorrect assumptions about payment status and mutability.","testable_assertion":"No entity with status 'confirmed' should exist in the system where payment has not been collected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A verbal agreement on a quote does NOT constitute payment and does NOT make the quote immutable. The customer can still back out.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A verbal agreement is not a financial transaction. Treating it as one — even linguistically — risks premature fulfillment, incorrect revenue recognition, and operational errors.","testable_assertion":"A quote in 'verbally agreed' status can still be modified, cancelled, or abandoned without financial consequence."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Domain terms must have exactly one meaning across all bounded contexts that share integration or reporting surfaces.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a DDD best practice for shared/published language rather than a physical constraint, but violating it causes severe operational confusion. A VP could theoretically approve the collision, but shouldn't.","testable_assertion":"Querying the system for all 'confirmed' entities returns a semantically homogeneous set — all paid and immutable."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A report queries all 'confirmed' items across quotes and orders. Some are paid/immutable, others are verbal/mutable.","expected_behavior":"This must never happen. Cross-context reporting on 'confirmed' must return semantically consistent results."},{"scenario":"A downstream fulfillment system receives a 'confirmed' event from the quotes context and begins picking/packing.","expected_behavior":"Premature fulfillment triggered by semantic collision. The quote is not paid — goods should not ship."},{"scenario":"Customer disputes: support agent sees 'confirmed' quote and tells customer the order is finalized and paid.","expected_behavior":"Miscommunication and potential legal/contractual issues. The customer only verbally agreed."},{"scenario":"A sales agent 'confirms' a quote, then the customer ghosts. The quote sits in 'confirmed' status indefinitely.","expected_behavior":"Unlike confirmed orders (which are immutable and paid), this 'confirmed' quote needs expiration, follow-up, and cancellation workflows — proving it is fundamentally different from a confirmed order."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK — Semantic collision could trigger premature revenue recognition or fulfillment of unpaid goods. If automated systems treat 'confirmed quote' like 'confirmed order', the business ships without payment.","operational":"HIGH RISK — Support, ops, and engineering teams will conflate the two meanings. Dashboards, alerts, and reports filtering on 'confirmed' become unreliable. Debugging incidents becomes harder when the same word means two different things.","user":"MEDIUM RISK — Sales agents may understand the distinction intuitively, but every other team (support, finance, warehouse) will be confused. New hires will absolutely conflate the terms.","risk":"This is a textbook example of domain language drift. The cost is not immediate — it is cumulative. Every future feature, integration, report, and conversation will bear the tax of disambiguation.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Low — they understand the verbal agreement context. But they lose clarity when discussing 'confirmed' items with other teams."},{"group":"Finance/Accounting","impact":"High — 'confirmed' implies payment received. A 'confirmed quote' without payment pollutes revenue reporting and recognition."},{"group":"Warehouse/Fulfillment","impact":"High — 'confirmed' is a fulfillment trigger. Semantic collision risks premature shipment."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Medium — agents referencing 'confirmed' status will make incorrect promises to customers about payment and order finality."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Medium — every developer touching cross-context code must remember that 'confirmed' means different things in different places. This is a permanent cognitive tax and bug source."}]},"confidence":{"level":90,"reasoning":"The existing domain definition is explicit and unambiguous. The feature request clearly redefines the same term with a contradictory meaning (unpaid vs paid, mutable vs immutable). This is not a judgment call — it is a direct semantic collision with well-understood consequences.","high_confidence_areas":["The term 'confirmed' is already defined and in production use","The proposed meaning directly contradicts the established meaning on two axes: payment status and mutability","Cross-context pollution is a concrete, predictable risk"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the Quotes and Orders contexts are truly separate bounded contexts with no shared integration surface (if fully isolated, the collision is less dangerous — but in practice, contexts are rarely fully isolated)"],"assumptions":["Quotes and Orders share at least some integration surface (reporting, events, or UI)","The existing definition of 'confirmed order' is enforced and relied upon by downstream systems"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposed 'confirmed' status for quotes directly contradicts the established domain definition of 'confirmed' in the orders context. Confirmed Order = paid + immutable. Proposed Confirmed Quote = unpaid + mutable. These are semantically opposite on the two most critical dimensions.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Fulfillment","Support","Engineering","Sales"],"options":["A) Reject the term 'confirmed' for quotes. Use 'accepted' or 'verbally agreed' instead.","B) Accept the collision and rely on context (quote vs order) to disambiguate.","C) Rename the existing order status to something else (extremely high cost, not recommended)."],"recommendation":"Option A — Use 'accepted' for quotes. It accurately describes the business event (customer accepted the terms), avoids collision with 'confirmed' (paid/immutable), and requires zero changes to the existing Orders domain. This is the lowest-cost, highest-clarity solution."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' is being redefined with a contradictory meaning across bounded contexts.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: 'Confirmed' means paid and immutable. The proposed quote status is neither."},{"violation":"Semantic pollution of cross-context reporting and automation surfaces.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: Domain terms must have exactly one meaning across shared integration surfaces."}]}
